subsection.title: Statement in forms
subsection.p: Let \omega=P\,dx+Q\,dy.

Since d\omega=(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy, this is
\boxed{ \int_{\partial R}P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_R (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy. }

subsection.title: Classical statement
Hypotheses

md.mrow: 1. \amp R\text{ is a bounded plane region with piecewise smooth boundary.}
3. \amp P\text{ and }Q\text{ have continuous first partial derivatives on a region containing }R.

subsection.title: Proof idea
subsection.p: First prove the theorem for one small rectangle.
For R=[a,a+h]\times[b,b+k], the line integral around the rectangle compares opposite sides.
Horizontal sides produce the term -P_y\,hk.
Vertical sides produce the term Q_x\,hk.

Together:
\int_{\partial R}P\,dx+Q\,dy \approx (Q_x-P_y)hk.
Then subdivide a larger region into many small rectangles.
Interior edges are walked twice in opposite directions, so their contributions cancel.
Only the outer boundary remains.
Let the mesh size go to zero.

subsection.title: What can fail
subsection.p: Orientation matters.
If the boundary is walked clockwise instead of counterclockwise, the sign changes.
The field must be defined on the whole region.

Example:
\omega= \frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\,dx + \frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\,dy.
This form is defined on \mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}.
On that punctured plane, d\omega=0.
But around the unit circle, \int_{C}\omega=2\pi.
Green's theorem cannot be applied to the disk bounded by C, because \omega is not defined at the origin.

subsection.title: Moral
subsection.p: Green's theorem is a cancellation theorem. Local rotation inside a region adds up to circulation around the boundary, as long as the field is smooth on the whole region.
